content:
5 min read
Just now

--

The goal is to build the simplest system that answers correctly, reliably, and economically.
LLMs are powerful, but they do not automatically know your private data.
That is why we use RAG: Retrieval-Augmented Generation.
RAG connects an LLM to external knowledge so it can answer from your documents, databases, tickets, policies, manuals, or enterprise systems.
But there is not just one kind of RAG.
There are three common patterns:

• Standard RAG

• Graph RAG

• Agentic RAG
Each one solves a different problem.
1. Standard RAG
Standard RAG is the simplest and most common approach.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The user asks a question. The query is converted into an embedding. That embedding is matched against a vector database. The top-K chunks are retrieved and passed to the LLM as context. The LLM then generates an answer from that context.
This works well when the answer exists directly inside documents.
Examples:

• HR policy search

• Product documentation Q&A

• Internal knowledge base chatbot

• Customer support assistant

• Developer docs search
Standard RAG is fast, cheap, and easy to operate.
But it has one major weakness: retrieval quality.
If the wrong chunks are retrieved, the answer will be wrong or incomplete. The system usually does not stop and ask, “Did I retrieve the right context?”
Use Standard RAG when speed matters and the answer lives clearly in your documents.
2. Graph RAG
Graph RAG is useful when relationships matter.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Instead of only searching text chunks, Graph RAG uses entities and relationships.
For example:
Customer → bought → Product
Product → depends on → Component
Component → has → Vulnerability
Policy → applies to → Region
This makes Graph RAG stronger for connected knowledge.
It usually supports two retrieval paths:

• Local search

• Global search
Local search for specific entity-based questions.
Example: “What risks are linked to Vendor X?”
The system finds the entity, traverses the knowledge graph, collects related context, and sends it to the LLM.
Global search for broad questions.
Example: “What are the major compliance risks across all documents?”
The system uses summarized communities or clusters of knowledge and asks the LLM to synthesize a high-level answer.
Graph RAG is useful for:

• Legal analysis

• Compliance mapping

• Biomedical research

• Fraud detection

• Supply chain risk

• Enterprise knowledge graphs
The trade-off is complexity.
Graph RAG is harder to build, slower to update, and more expensive to maintain. You need entity extraction, relationship extraction, graph construction, indexing, summarization, and refresh pipelines.
Use Graph RAG when the answer depends on connected knowledge, not just similar text.
3. Agentic RAG
Agentic RAG adds reasoning to retrieval.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Instead of retrieving once and answering, an agent plans the retrieval process.
The agent reads the query, breaks it into sub-questions, chooses the right tools or sources, retrieves context, evaluates whether the context is enough, and re-retrieves if needed.
Then the LLM writes the final answer.
This is useful for complex questions.
Example:
“Compare Q2 churn drivers with support ticket themes and identify which product issues are affecting enterprise renewals.”
A simple RAG system may retrieve a few chunks and guess.
An Agentic RAG system can break the task into steps:

• Find churn drivers.

• Search support tickets.

• Identify product issues.

• Compare against renewal data.

• Evaluate evidence.

• Synthesize the final answer.
Agentic RAG is useful for:

• Incident investigation

• Financial research

• Data platform copilots

• Complex customer support

• Engineering assistants

• Cross-system enterprise search
The trade-off is cost and observability.
Agentic RAG may require multiple LLM calls, retrieval calls, tool calls, evaluations, and retries. That makes it slower, more expensive, and harder to debug.
Use Agentic RAG when the task needs multi-step reasoning, source selection, and self-correction.
Quick Comparison
Standard RAG is fast and cheap. Use it when answers live directly in documents.
Graph RAG is better for connected knowledge. Use it when relationships between entities matter.
Agentic RAG is more flexible. Use it when the question requires planning, multiple sources, and self-correction.
Practical Rule
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Start with Standard RAG.
Add reranking, citations, chunk quality, metadata filters, evaluation, and observability.
Move to Graph RAG when retrieval fails because relationships matter.
Move to Agentic RAG when retrieval fails because the task requires reasoning and source selection.
Do not start with complexity.
Earn it.
Final Thought
Press enter or click to view image in full size

RAG connects LLMs to your data.
Standard RAG gives the model relevant text.
Graph RAG gives the model connected knowledge.
Agentic RAG gives the model a reasoning loop.
None of them is universally better.
The right architecture depends on the problem.

• If the question is simple, use Standard RAG.

• If the knowledge is connected, use Graph RAG.

• If the task is multi-step, use Agentic RAG.
In enterprise AI, the goal is not to build the most advanced architecture.
The goal is to build the simplest system that answers correctly, reliably, and economically.
Complexity is not the trophy.
Trust is.
